results.possibleIdentity: Robert Bruce Newman (commonly known as Robert Newman) was a conceptual and multimedia artist active in the New York City art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for founding Gain Ground in 1969, which was one of the first influential 'alternative' art spaces that provided a venue for experimental and technological art forms, including early works by Vito Acconci. Newman's own artistic practice often combined language, photography, and technology; his notable works include 'Signs' (1967), an early example of 'bookwork art' exhibited at the Dwan Gallery's 'Language' show, and immersive installations such as 'Rooms with Electric Mirrors' (1967) and 'Power Throne' (1968), the latter of which used heart sounds in a series of chambers. He was associated with the era's experimentation in art and technology, being mentioned in the archives of prominent E.A.T. (Experiments in Art and Technology) figures like Newton Harrison. In addition to his creative work, he served as the director of the Spectrum Gallery and was an art critic for 'Arts Magazine'.

Robert Newman (born 1905, died 1983) was a co-founder of the influential acoustics and technology firm Bolt, Beranek and Newman (BBN). While primarily a scientist and an expert in architectural acoustics (serving as a professor at MIT), Newman was an active participant in the 'art and technology' circles of the 1960s and 1970s. He was a collector of contemporary art and was involved with the Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS) at MIT. His firm, BBN, often collaborated with artists and engineers on multimedia projects related to Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), particularly in the field of sound and acoustic environments. His interdisciplinary work between engineering and the arts made him a key figure in the historical development of technological art institutions.

results.possibleIdentity: Gilberto (Gil) Gerald is a Panamanian-born architect, designer, and prominent activist for LGBTQ+ rights and HIV/AIDS advocacy, active from the late 1960s to the present. He arrived in the United States in 1967 and studied architecture at Pratt Institute from 1968 to 1974, where he was involved with the Delta Gamma Theta (DGT) alumni association. During this period, he was part of the collaborative artistic and technical circles in New York, notably working as an architectural designer for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His work with E.A.T. included contributing to the design and development of the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. Gerald's later career shifted significantly toward civil rights; in 1978, he co-founded the National Coalition of Black Gays (now the National Coalition of Black Lesbians and Gays) and served as its Executive Director starting in 1983. He was also an incorporator of the Human Rights Campaign Fund (HRC) and held leadership roles at the National AIDS Network and the Minority AIDS Project, where he became a leading voice in addressing the impact of the HIV/AIDS epidemic on Black and minority communities. He currently resides in Cathedral City, California.
